gomutants self-efficacy (v0.1.0)
================================

Mutation testing result when gomutants is run on its own test suite.
Measured per-package (baselines stay small) with 10 workers, darwin/arm64.

Package              Total  Killed  Lived  NotCov  NotViable  TimedOut  Efficacy
-----------------    -----  ------  -----  ------  ---------  --------  --------
internal/patch          19      11      7       0          1         0    61.11%
internal/config         42      41      1       0          0         0    97.62%
internal/mutator        98      75     13       0         10         0    85.23%
internal/report         98      58     36       0          4         0    61.70%
internal/discover       72      38     20       0         12         2    65.52%
internal/coverage      144      98     24       1         15         6    80.33%
internal/runner         80      43     21       9          6         1    67.19%
main                   111      36     55       8          2        10    39.56%
-----------------    -----  ------  -----  ------  ---------  --------  --------
TOTAL                  664     400    177      18         50        19    69.32%

Mutations coverage: 97.29%  (646 of 664 mutants were exercised by tests)
Test efficacy:      69.32%  (killed / (killed + lived))

Method
------
Per-package runs, not `./...`, because the full-repo baseline (~41s) gives a
per-mutant timeout 100x larger than most packages actually need. Per-package
baselines are 0.3s–7s, which keeps total wall-clock under 15 minutes.

Caveat: per-package coverage is narrower than `./...`. A line in internal/X
exercised only by internal/Y's tests will count as "not covered" here but
would be covered under `./...`. Expect the aggregate to mildly under-count
coverage and killed mutants relative to a true full-repo run.

Observations
------------
- main (39.56%) drags the total down. Integration tests in main_test.go
  and integration_test.go invoke the full gomutants pipeline, so many
  mutations (especially in output formatting and error wrapping) survive
  because the integration tests don't assert on those signals.
- patch (61.11%) and report (61.70%) have many lived mutants in formatting
  and edge-case arithmetic that exhaustive unit tests don't pin down.
- config (97.62%) is the strongest — config.Load/ApplyFlags have tight
  table-driven tests that catch almost every mutation.

Interpretation
--------------
100% statement coverage ≠ high mutation efficacy. Self-efficacy 69.32%
documents a real test-quality gap — there are mutations we don't catch.
This is a baseline for v0.1.0. Improving test strength (especially in
main and report) would raise this number without changing production code.
